examples.title: Hamming Code Generation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to generate Hamming code by inserting parity bits at positions that are powers of 2
examples.code:
ALGORITHM HammingCodeGeneration(dataBits[], r)
// r is number of redundant bits
// dataBits is the original data (2^r - r - 1 bits)
BEGIN
 n = LENGTH(dataBits)
 codeword = ARRAY[2^r]
 parityPos = 1
 dataPos = 3
 // Place parity bits at positions 1,2,4,8...
 FOR i FROM 0 TO 2^r-1 DO
 IF i+1 == parityPos THEN
 codeword[i] = 0 // Initialize parity to 0
 parityPos = parityPos * 2
 ELSE
 codeword[i] = dataBits[dataPos-3]
 dataPos = dataPos + 1
 END IF
 END FOR
 // Calculate parity bits
 FOR p FROM 0 TO r-1 DO
 parityPos = POWER(2, p)
 parity = 0
 FOR j FROM parityPos-1 TO 2^r-1 DO
 IF (j+1) AND parityPos != 0 THEN
 parity = parity XOR codeword[j]
 END IF
 END FOR
 codeword[parityPos-1] = parity
 END FOR
 RETURN codeword
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm inserts parity bits at positions 1,2,4,8... (powers of 2) and calculates each parity bit by XORing specific data bits based on position. The final codeword provides single-bit error correction capability.

examples.title: CRC Generation Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to generate CRC bits using polynomial division
examples.code:
ALGORITHM CRCGeneration(data[], generator[])
// data[]: original data bits
// generator[]: generator polynomial bits (degree = m)
BEGIN
 m = LENGTH(generator) - 1
 // Append m zeros to data
 extendedData = CONCATENATE(data, ZEROS(m))
 remainder = DIVIDE(extendedData, generator)
 // Append remainder to original data
 crcCode = CONCATENATE(data, remainder)
 RETURN crcCode
END

ALGORITHM DIVIDE(dividend[], divisor[])
BEGIN
 remainder = COPY(dividend)
 FOR i FROM 0 TO LENGTH(dividend) - LENGTH(divisor) DO
 IF remainder[i] == 1 THEN
 FOR j FROM 0 TO LENGTH(divisor)-1 DO
 remainder[i+j] = remainder[i+j] XOR divisor[j]
 END FOR
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN remainder[LENGTH(remainder)-m TO END]
END
examples.explanation: CRC uses polynomial long division. The sender appends zeros (equal to generator degree), performs division, and appends the remainder. The receiver performs the same division - if remainder is zero, no error is detected.

examples.title: Go-Back-N ARQ Sender Protocol
examples.description: Pseudocode for sender side of Go-Back-N ARQ protocol
examples.code:
ALGORITHM GoBackNSender(frame)
BEGIN
 windowSize = N
 base = 0
 nextSeqNum = 0
 WHILE moreFrames DO
 IF nextSeqNum < base + windowSize THEN
 SEND(frame, nextSeqNum)
 IF base == nextSeqNum THEN
 START_TIMER(base) // Start timer for oldest unACKed frame
 END IF
 nextSeqNum = nextSeqNum + 1
 ELSE
 // Window full - wait for ACKs
 WAIT_FOR_ACK()
 END IF
 // Process incoming ACKs
 IF ACK(n) RECEIVED THEN
 base = n + 1 // Move window base forward
 IF base == nextSeqNum THEN
 STOP_TIMER()
 ELSE
 START_TIMER(base)
 END IF
 END IF
 IF TIMEOUT THEN
 // Retransmit all frames from base to nextSeqNum-1
 nextSeqNum = base
 FOR i FROM base TO nextSeqNum-1 DO
 RESEND(frame[i], i)
 END FOR
 START_TIMER(base)
 END IF
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: Go-Back-N sender maintains a window of unacknowledged frames. When timeout occurs, ALL frames from the base are retransmitted. Cumulative ACKs acknowledge all frames up to a sequence number.
